title: Rice and waxy brassica foliar delivery starter scope
citation: Project-authored starter document for FoliarShield-AI retrieval workflow validation; replace with license-compatible public literature before benchmarking.
abstract: FoliarShield-AI starts with rice as an agriculturally relevant cereal leaf and a waxy brassica or cabbage-type leaf as a difficult-to-wet hydrophobic benchmark. Fast assay labels include droplet impact, contact-line motion, spray coverage, retained fluorescent intensity, wash-off, evaporation, and early release. Living-payload assays remain contained and review-gated.

title: Cloaked droplets and liquid-liquid encapsulation starter scope
citation: Project-authored starter document for FoliarShield-AI retrieval workflow validation; replace with license-compatible public literature before benchmarking.
abstract: The formulation search space includes cloaked droplets, liquid-liquid encapsulation, polymer-assisted emulsions, release-trigger materials, and controlled-release shell architectures. The first optimization loop should use nonliving payload labels for retention and release before testing Bacillus-like payload viability under contained conditions.

title: Batched active learning starter scope
citation: Project-authored starter document for FoliarShield-AI retrieval workflow validation; replace with license-compatible public literature before benchmarking.
abstract: FoliarShield-AI uses a batched, tiered discovery loop. Surrogate models estimate outcomes and uncertainty from fast physical assays, then Bayesian optimization proposes the next formulation batch under safety, feasibility, sprayability, material, and responsible-use constraints. The LLM agent retrieves evidence, explains hypotheses, checks constraints, and prepares auditable rationales for human review.

title: Enhancing spray retention using cloaked droplets to reduce pesticide pollution
citation: Jayaprakash, V., Rufer, S., Panat, S. and Varanasi, K. K. Enhancing spray retention using cloaked droplets to reduce pesticide pollution. Soft Matter 21, 3688-3699 (2025). https://doi.org/10.1039/D4SM01496K
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this open-access paper directly supports oil-cloaked droplets as a foliar retention strategy. It reports high-speed droplet impact measurements, oil fraction and viscosity sweeps, superhydrophobic model surfaces, cabbage leaf spray coverage tests, rebound suppression, and reduced spray waste. FoliarShield should map this record to droplet impact video endpoints, spray coverage images, hydrophobic-leaf retention, and oil-cloak feasibility constraints.

title: Tunable encapsulation of sessile droplets with solid and liquid shells
citation: Lathia, R., Nagpal, S., Dey Modak, C. et al. Tunable encapsulation of sessile droplets with solid and liquid shells. Nature Communications 14, 6445 (2023). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-41977-1
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this open-access paper supports liquid-liquid and solid-shell encapsulation concepts for sessile droplets. It describes capillary-force-assisted cloaking with hydrophobic colloidal particles and liquid-infused surfaces, tunable shell thickness, evaporation-rate control, and external-trigger uncoating or merging. FoliarShield should use it as physics evidence for controllable shell thickness, evaporation endpoints, and release-trigger hypotheses, while requiring foliar compatibility tests before candidate promotion.

title: Polymer additives regulate the deposition behavior of pesticide droplets on target plants
citation: Song, Y., Huang, G., Zheng, L. et al. Polymer additives regulate the deposition behavior of pesticide droplets on target plants. Polymer Testing 93, 106958 (2021). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymertesting.2020.106958
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this open-access article supports polymer additive screening for foliar deposition. It compares polymer additives through liquid-property measurements, dynamic contact angles, and high-speed simulated field impact on hydrophilic and hydrophobic leaves. FoliarShield should treat polymer additives as sprayability and deposition modifiers, not as automatically beneficial materials, because dynamic impact outcomes can diverge from static wetting observations.

title: Deposition and water repelling of temperature-responsive nanopesticides on leaves
citation: Tang, J., Tong, X., Chen, Y. et al. Deposition and water repelling of temperature-responsive nanopesticides on leaves. Nature Communications 14, 6401 (2023). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-41878-3
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this open-access paper supports foliar nanopesticide deposition and anti-washoff endpoints. It motivates adhesion to foliage, rain erosion resistance, leaf water-repelling behavior, and temperature-responsive formulation behavior. FoliarShield should map this record to simulated rain, retained intensity, material safety, and trigger-responsive release objectives while requiring payload-specific compatibility review.

title: Dispersal of Bacillus subtilis and its effect on strawberry phyllosphere microbiota under open field and protection conditions
citation: Wei, F., Hu, X. and Xu, X. Dispersal of Bacillus subtilis and its effect on strawberry phyllosphere microbiota under open field and protection conditions. Scientific Reports 6, 22611 (2016). https://doi.org/10.1038/srep22611
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this open-access paper supports living-payload persistence and phyllosphere monitoring endpoints. It studies Bacillus subtilis dispersal after application on strawberry plants under open-field and protected conditions and links efficacy variability to dispersal and host-tissue growth. FoliarShield should use this as evidence that Bacillus-like payload promotion requires contained viability, persistence, dispersal, and microbiome-impact review gates.

title: Suppression of Magnaporthe oryzae and interaction between Bacillus subtilis and rice plants in the control of rice blast
citation: Sha, Y., Wang, Q. and Li, Y. Suppression of Magnaporthe oryzae and interaction between Bacillus subtilis and rice plants in the control of rice blast. SpringerPlus 5, 1238 (2016). https://doi.org/10.1186/s40064-016-2858-1
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this open-access paper supports rice as the first cereal leaf panel for contained Bacillus-like foliar checks. It examines Bacillus subtilis strains on rice leaves, pathogen suppression, colonization, and plant defense enzyme responses. FoliarShield should connect this evidence to rice-leaf persistence, living-payload viability, and plant-response labels only after responsible-use review.

title: Bayesian optimization with active learning of design constraints using an entropy-based approach
citation: Khatamsaz, D., Vela, B., Singh, P. et al. Bayesian optimization with active learning of design constraints using an entropy-based approach. npj Computational Materials 9, 49 (2023). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41524-023-01006-7
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this open-access paper supports active learning when candidate designs must satisfy multiple constraints. It frames materials design as an optimization problem with objectives and design constraints, using uncertainty-aware model updates to guide experiment selection. FoliarShield should map this to material availability, sprayability, biosafety review, and contained-use approval constraints in the batched optimizer.

title: Parallel Bayesian Optimization of Multiple Noisy Objectives with Expected Hypervolume Improvement
citation: Daulton, S., Balandat, M. and Bakshy, E. Parallel Bayesian Optimization of Multiple Noisy Objectives with Expected Hypervolume Improvement. NeurIPS (2021). arXiv:2105.08195. https://arxiv.org/abs/2105.08195
abstract: Project-authored literature note: this paper supports batch multi-objective acquisition for noisy experimental labels. It introduces noisy expected hypervolume improvement and a parallel variant for selecting multiple candidates under multiple objectives. FoliarShield should use it as the acquisition-method reference for retention, wash-off, release, sprayability, viability, safety, and cost tradeoffs once fixture scores are replaced by measured assay endpoints.
